questions.questionText: The parietal cells of the stomach secrete:
questions.explanation: Parietal (oxyntic) cells: HCl (via H+/K+ ATPase — proton pump) and intrinsic factor (essential for B12 absorption in ileum). Chief cells: pepsinogen (→ pepsin in acid). G cells: gastrin. Mucous cells: mucus + HCO3- (mucosal barrier). Loss of parietal cells → achlorhydria + pernicious anemia (no IF → B12 deficiency). PPIs (omeprazole) block H+/K+ ATPase. Reference: Guyton — Gastric Secretion.

questions.questionText: Gastrin is secreted by G cells of the stomach antrum and stimulates:
questions.optionB: HCl secretion, gastric motility, and growth of gastric mucosa
questions.explanation: Gastrin: secreted by G cells (antrum, duodenum). Stimulates parietal cells (HCl secretion via CCK-B receptors), chief cells, gastric motility, and trophic effects on GI mucosa. Stimulated by: protein/peptides in stomach, vagal stimulation (GRP), gastric distension. Inhibited by: low pH (<2), somatostatin. Zollinger-Ellison syndrome: gastrinoma → massive HCl secretion → peptic ulcers. Reference: Guyton — Gastrin.

questions.questionText: The migrating motor complex (MMC) is:
questions.optionB: Cyclical pattern of GI motility during fasting (housekeeping function)
questions.explanation: MMC: cyclical motor pattern during fasting (interdigestive period) occurring every 90-120 minutes. 4 phases: Phase I (quiescence), Phase II (irregular), Phase III (intense regular contractions — 'housekeeper wave' — sweeps food debris and bacteria from stomach and small intestine distally), Phase IV (transition). Regulated by motilin. Disrupted after eating. Erythromycin (motilin agonist) stimulates MMC. Reference: Guyton — GI Motility.

questions.questionText: Secretin is released from S cells of the duodenum in response to:
questions.explanation: Secretin: released by S cells in duodenum when pH < 4.5 (acid chyme enters). Actions: stimulates pancreatic HCO3- secretion (duct cells), stimulates bile secretion (choleretic), inhibits gastric HCl secretion and gastric motility. CCK is released by fat and protein in duodenum → stimulates pancreatic enzyme secretion and gallbladder contraction. Secretin and CCK potentiate each other's effects on pancreas. Reference: Guyton — Secretin.

questions.questionText: CCK (cholecystokinin) is released by I cells in response to amino acids and fatty acids, and causes:
questions.optionB: Gallbladder contraction, pancreatic enzyme secretion, and satiety
questions.explanation: CCK (from I cells in duodenum/jejunum): (1) Gallbladder contraction + sphincter of Oddi relaxation → bile release, (2) Pancreatic enzyme secretion (acinar cells), (3) Delay gastric emptying, (4) Satiety signal (CNS), (5) Trophic effect on pancreas. Stimulated by: fatty acids, amino acids, and peptides in duodenum. CCK and secretin potentiate each other. Reference: Guyton — CCK.

questions.questionText: Bile acids are reabsorbed primarily in the:
questions.explanation: Bile acid enterohepatic circulation: bile acids secreted into duodenum → emulsify fats → reabsorbed in terminal ileum (by ASBT — apical sodium-dependent bile acid transporter) → portal vein → liver → re-secreted. ~95% recycled, ~5% lost in feces (replaced by de novo synthesis from cholesterol). Bile acid pool recycles 6-8 times/day. Ileal resection → bile acid malabsorption → fat malabsorption, diarrhea. Reference: Guyton — Enterohepatic Circulation.

questions.questionText: Vitamin B12 absorption requires:
questions.optionB: Intrinsic factor (from parietal cells) and absorption in the terminal ileum
questions.explanation: B12 absorption: dietary B12 bound to food proteins → released by gastric acid and pepsin → binds R-protein (haptocorrin from saliva) → in duodenum, pancreatic proteases release B12 from R-protein → B12 binds intrinsic factor (IF) → IF-B12 complex absorbed in terminal ileum via cubilin receptor. Causes of B12 deficiency: pernicious anemia (anti-IF antibodies), gastrectomy, ileal resection, Crohn's, Diphyllobothrium latum. Reference: Guyton — B12 Absorption.

questions.questionText: The primary site of iron absorption is the:
questions.explanation: Iron absorption: mainly duodenum (and upper jejunum). Fe3+ reduced to Fe2+ by ferrireductase (DcytB) and vitamin C → enters enterocyte via DMT1 → stored as ferritin or exported via ferroportin → Fe2+ oxidized to Fe3+ by hephaestin → binds transferrin. Hepcidin (from liver) degrades ferroportin → master regulator. Factors enhancing: acid, vitamin C, meat (heme iron). Inhibiting: phytates, tannins, calcium. Reference: Guyton — Iron Absorption.

questions.questionText: The gastrocolic reflex is triggered by:
questions.optionB: Food entering the stomach → increases colonic motility (mass movements)
questions.explanation: Gastrocolic reflex: distension of stomach (especially by lipid-rich meals) → sends signals via vagus and enteric nervous system → increased colonic motility and mass movements → urge to defecate. Most active after breakfast (longest fasting period). Mediated by gastrin, CCK, and parasympathetic activation. Gastroileal reflex: gastric stimulation increases ileocecal valve opening. These are long reflexes coordinating upper and lower GI function. Reference: Guyton — GI Reflexes.

questions.questionText: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) digests:
questions.explanation: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) initiates starch digestion by cleaving α-1,4 glycosidic bonds → produces maltose, maltotriose, and α-limit dextrins. Optimal pH 6.8 (neutral). Inactivated by gastric acid. Pancreatic amylase continues starch digestion in duodenum. Final digestion by brush border enzymes (maltase, isomaltase, sucrase, lactase) → monosaccharides absorbed via SGLT1 (glucose/galactose) and GLUT5 (fructose). Reference: Guyton — Carbohydrate Digestion.

questions.questionText: The interstitial cells of Cajal (ICC) function as:
questions.optionB: Pacemaker cells of the GI tract (generate slow waves/BER)
questions.explanation: ICCs are the pacemaker cells of the GI tract, generating slow waves (basic electrical rhythm/BER). Slow wave frequency: stomach 3/min, duodenum 12/min (highest), ileum 8/min. Slow waves are not action potentials — they set the maximum frequency of contractions. When slow waves reach threshold → spike potentials → contraction. ICCs are located between circular and longitudinal muscle layers (at the Auerbach plexus). Reference: Guyton — GI Slow Waves.

questions.questionText: Fat digestion is primarily accomplished by:
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase (with colipase as cofactor) is the most important enzyme for fat digestion. It cleaves triglycerides into 2 fatty acids + 2-monoglyceride at the oil-water interface (bile salts emulsify fat → increase surface area). Products form mixed micelles with bile salts → absorbed in jejunum. Gastric lipase contributes ~10%. Colipase anchors lipase to the lipid droplet despite bile salt coating. Reference: Guyton — Fat Digestion.

questions.questionText: The most potent stimulus for gallbladder contraction is:
questions.explanation: CCK is the most potent physiological stimulus for gallbladder contraction. Released by fats and proteins in duodenum. CCK also relaxes the sphincter of Oddi → bile flows into duodenum. Vagal stimulation also causes mild gallbladder contraction. Somatostatin inhibits gallbladder contraction (promotes stasis → increases gallstone risk in somatostatin-producing tumors and octreotide therapy). Reference: Guyton — Gallbladder.

questions.questionText: The enteric nervous system is called the 'little brain' because it:
questions.optionB: Can function independently of the CNS, containing its own sensory, motor, and interneurons
questions.explanation: The enteric nervous system (ENS, ~100 million neurons): 2 plexuses — Auerbach's (myenteric, between muscle layers — motility) and Meissner's (submucosal — secretion, blood flow). Functions independently (peristalsis continues in denervated gut). Modulated by sympathetic (inhibits) and parasympathetic (stimulates). Contains senosry neurons, interneurons, motor neurons. Uses ACh, VIP, NO, substance P, 5-HT. Reference: Guyton — ENS.

questions.questionText: Most water absorption in the GI tract occurs in the:
questions.explanation: Total fluid entering GI tract daily: ~9L (2L oral + 7L secretions). Small intestine absorbs ~7-8L (~80-90%). Colon absorbs ~1-1.5L. Stool water: ~100-200 mL/day. Jejunum: most absorption (paracellular, driven by solute absorption). Ileum and colon: tighter junctions, can absorb against gradient. Cholera toxin → massive small intestinal secretion → secretory diarrhea (up to 20L/day). ORS works because glucose-coupled Na+ absorption remains intact. Reference: Guyton — GI Water Absorption.

questions.questionText: The phases of gastric acid secretion are:
questions.explanation: Three phases: (1) Cephalic (~30%): sight, smell, taste, thought of food → vagus nerve → ACh → parietal cells. (2) Gastric (~60%): food distension → gastrin release from G cells → HCl; protein buffers → raise pH → more gastrin. (3) Intestinal (~10%): amino acids in duodenum → mild stimulation. Inhibition: low pH (<2) → somatostatin → inhibits gastrin. Enterogastric reflex inhibits gastric acid when chyme enters duodenum. Reference: Guyton — Gastric Acid Phases.

questions.questionText: GIP (Glucose-dependent Insulinotropic Peptide) is an incretin that:
questions.optionB: Potentiates insulin release in response to oral glucose
questions.explanation: GIP (from K cells in duodenum/jejunum): stimulates insulin secretion from β cells in a glucose-dependent manner (incretin effect → oral glucose produces more insulin than IV glucose at same blood glucose level). Also inhibits gastric acid secretion (hence original name 'gastric inhibitory peptide'). GLP-1 (from L cells in ileum/colon) is the other incretin. DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin) prevent GIP/GLP-1 breakdown → used in diabetes. Reference: Guyton — Incretins.

questions.questionText: Bilirubin metabolism involves conjugation in the liver by:
questions.explanation: Bilirubin metabolism: Heme → biliverdin → unconjugated bilirubin (indirect, water-insoluble, bound to albumin) → liver uptake → conjugation by UGT (UDP-glucuronyl transferase) with glucuronic acid → conjugated bilirubin (direct, water-soluble) → excreted in bile → intestinal bacteria convert to urobilinogen → stercobilinogen → stercobilin (brown stool color). Gilbert syndrome: reduced UGT activity. Crigler-Najjar: absent/severely reduced UGT. Reference: Guyton — Bilirubin.

questions.questionText: The primary function of the colon is:
questions.optionB: Absorption of water and electrolytes, storage and elimination of feces
questions.explanation: Colon functions: (1) Water and electrolyte absorption (~1-1.5 L/day), (2) Storage and compaction of feces, (3) Bacterial fermentation (produces SCFAs — butyrate is main fuel for colonocytes, vitamin K, some B vitamins), (4) Mucus secretion (lubrication). Minimal digestion occurs. Mass movements (1-3 times/day) propel contents toward rectum. Defecation reflex: rectal distension → parasympathetic → internal sphincter relaxation. Reference: Guyton — Colonic Function.

questions.questionText: Lower esophageal sphincter (LES) tone is INCREASED by:
questions.explanation: LES tone increased by: metoclopramide, cisapride, domperidone (prokinetics), gastrin, motilin, substance P, α-adrenergic agonists. LES tone decreased by: smoking, alcohol, chocolate, coffee, fatty meals, CCK, secretin, VIP, NO, progesterone (pregnancy → GERD), calcium channel blockers, nitrates, theophylline. Low LES tone → GERD. High LES tone (failure to relax) → achalasia. Reference: Guyton — LES.

questions.questionText: Receptive relaxation of the stomach is mediated by:
questions.optionB: Vagus nerve (VIP and NO as neurotransmitters)
questions.explanation: Receptive relaxation: as food enters the stomach, the fundus relaxes to accommodate the bolus without significant increase in intragastric pressure. This is a vagovagal reflex mediated by non-adrenergic, non-cholinergic (NANC) neurons releasing VIP and NO. Allows the stomach to store up to 1.5L without significant pressure increase. Vagotomy abolishes receptive relaxation → rapid increase in gastric pressure → early satiety. Reference: Guyton — Gastric Relaxation.

questions.questionText: Lactose intolerance results from deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Lactase (brush border enzyme in jejunum) cleaves lactose → glucose + galactose. Lactase deficiency (most common disaccharidase deficiency worldwide) → undigested lactose reaches colon → bacterial fermentation → gas (CO2, H2, CH4), bloating, osmotic diarrhea. Diagnosed by hydrogen breath test (elevated H2 after lactose ingestion). Primary (adult-onset, genetic, most common). Secondary (mucosal damage — celiac, infections). Reference: Guyton — Lactose Intolerance.

questions.questionText: Somatostatin is a universal inhibitor of GI function. It is secreted by:
questions.optionB: D cells throughout the GI tract and pancreatic islets
questions.explanation: Somatostatin (from D cells in GI tract and δ cells in pancreatic islets): inhibits almost everything — gastrin, HCl, pepsin, pancreatic secretion, insulin, glucagon, bile secretion, GI motility, gallbladder contraction, intestinal absorption. Called the 'universal inhibitor.' Octreotide (somatostatin analog) used for acromegaly, carcinoid, VIPoma, variceal bleeding. Released by local acid → negative feedback on gastrin. Reference: Guyton — Somatostatin.

questions.questionText: The short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) produced by colonic bacteria are mainly absorbed as fuel by:
questions.explanation: SCFAs (acetate, propionate, butyrate) are produced by bacterial fermentation of undigested carbohydrates (fiber) in the colon. Butyrate is the preferred energy source for colonocytes (provides ~70% of their energy needs). Acetate enters systemic circulation → fuel for muscles. Propionate → liver (gluconeogenesis). SCFAs also stimulate Na+ and water absorption, maintain colonic mucosal health. Fiber deficiency → reduced SCFAs → increased colon cancer risk. Reference: Guyton — SCFAs.
